theory.intro: The best graph for a dataset depends on the type of data and what you want to show. Match the data and its purpose to the display that shows it most clearly.
facts: **Categorical** data, when comparing categories, suits a **column (bar) graph**.
**Parts of a whole** (each category as a share of a total) suit a **sector (pie) graph** or divided bar graph.
**Change over time** with continuous data suits a **line graph**.
The **shape** of a numerical dataset (clusters, gaps, outliers) suits a **dot plot**, **stem-and-leaf plot** or **histogram**.
steps: Identify the type of data (categorical or numerical).
Identify the purpose (compare categories, show a distribution, show change over time, or show parts of a whole).
Match the data and purpose to the display that shows it best.

foundation.question_text:
A scientist measures a seedling’s height at the end of each day for two weeks and wants to show how the height changes over the fortnight.
Which type of graph is most suitable?
foundation.solution_text:
The purpose is to show change over time with continuous data.
A line graph is most suitable.

foundation.question_text:
A shop counts how many of each ice-cream flavour it sold and wants to compare the flavours.
Which type of graph is most suitable?
foundation.solution_text:
The data is categorical and the purpose is to compare categories.
A column (bar) graph is most suitable.

foundation.question_text:
You want to show what share of a monthly budget goes to rent, food and transport, out of the whole budget.
Which type of graph is most suitable?
foundation.solution_text:
The purpose is to show parts of a whole.
A sector (pie) graph is most suitable.

foundation.question_text:
A teacher wants to show the clusters and any outliers in the $30$ test scores of a class.
Which type of graph is most suitable?
foundation.solution_text:
The data is numerical and the purpose is to show its shape (clusters and outliers).
A dot plot is most suitable (a stem-and-leaf plot would also work).

foundation.question_text: Explain why a line graph is a suitable choice for showing a city’s temperature measured every hour through the day.
foundation.solution_text:
Temperature over time is continuous data that changes over time.
A line graph shows that change and lets you estimate values between the readings.

development.question_text:
A canteen wants a display showing the actual number of each drink sold this week, not the percentages.
Column graph or sector graph? Justify your choice.
development.solution_text:
A sector graph shows shares of a whole, not exact frequencies.
Choose a column graph, because it shows the actual number sold for each drink and makes them easy to compare.

development.question_text:
A student wants to display their classmates’ favourite sports using a line graph.
Explain why a line graph is not appropriate for this data.
development.solution_text:
Favourite sport is categorical data.
A line graph joins points to show change between them, but there is nothing between "soccer" and "netball", so a column graph should be used instead.

development.question_text:
A set of $60$ exam marks ranges from $31$ to $98$, and you want a display that shows the shape of the data while still keeping every individual mark.
Which numerical display is most suitable?
development.solution_text:
The purpose is to show the shape of numerical data over a large range while keeping each value.
A stem-and-leaf plot is most suitable, because it groups the marks by tens yet still lists every mark.

development.question_text:
A charity wants a single display that shows how its total spending is split between four programs, as a share of the whole.
Which type of graph is most suitable, and why?
development.solution_text:
The purpose is to show parts of a whole.
A sector graph is most suitable, because each sector shows one program’s share of the total spending.

mastery.question_text:
A café has its total sales for each month of the year. Name the most suitable graph for each purpose below and justify each choice.
(a) To show the trend in sales across the year.
(b) To show each month’s sales as a share of the year’s total.
mastery.solution_text:
(a) A line graph, because it shows change over time across the year.
(b) A sector graph, because it shows each month as a part of the whole year’s sales.

mastery.question_text:
The shoe sizes of a class are recorded (a numerical, discrete dataset) and you want to see the most common size and the spread. A student chooses a line graph.
Explain why the line graph is a poor choice and name a better display.
mastery.solution_text:
The data is not a change over time, so joining the points with a line is meaningless.
A dot plot is a better choice, because it shows the most common size and the spread of a numerical dataset.
